title: Adjective Agreement
intro: In Spanish, adjectives must match the gender and number of the noun they describe. This is called agreement — and it's one of the most important rules in the language.

sections.title: Adjectives ending in -E or consonant (2 forms — only plural changes)
sections.content:
These adjectives are the same for masculine and feminine:
• grande (big): el coche grande / la casa grande
• inteligente (intelligent): el chico inteligente / la chica inteligente
• fácil (easy): el libro fácil / la tarea fácil
For plural, just add -s (vowel) or -es (consonant):
• grande → grandes, fácil → fáciles

sections.title: Where do adjectives go?
sections.content:
In Spanish, adjectives usually come AFTER the noun:
• un coche rojo (a red car)
• una casa grande (a big house)
Some short, common adjectives can come before: buen (good), mal (bad), gran (great)

items.es: El libro rojo está en la mesa.
items.en: The red book is on the table.

questions.explanation: 'interesante' ends in -e — same for masculine and feminine! ✓
fácil ends in consonant, plural → add -es → fáciles ✓

questions.prompt: Where does the adjective usually go in Spanish?
questions.explanation: Adjectives usually come AFTER the noun in Spanish: un coche rojo (a red car) ✓
